title: The AI Scientist-v2: Workshop-Level Automated Scientific Discovery via Agentic Tree Search
authors: Yutaro Yamada et al. (Sakana AI, UBC, Vector, Oxford)
contribution: End-to-end agentic system using progressive tree search; first entirely AI-generated paper to pass ICLR workshop peer review (scoring 6.33, top ~45%). Cost: ~$15/paper. Automated Reviewer achieved 69% balanced accuracy.

title: Towards an AI co-scientist
authors: Juraj Gottweis et al. (Google Research/DeepMind, 34 authors)
venue: arXiv Feb 2025; featured in Nature Medicine
contribution: Multi-agent system on Gemini 2.0 using generate-debate-evolve; validated with wet-lab results in drug repurposing (AML candidates showing tumor inhibition) and novel liver fibrosis targets.

title: AI-Researcher: Autonomous Scientific Innovation
authors: Jiabin Tang, Lianghao Xia, Zhonghang Li, Chao Huang (HKU)
contribution: Fully autonomous research system orchestrating literature review -> idea generation -> implementation -> manuscript; introduces Scientist-Bench.

title: Agent Laboratory: Using LLM Agents as Research Assistants
authors: Samuel Schmidgall et al. (AMD, JHU, ETH Zurich)
contribution: PhD/Postdoc/ML Engineer agents completing research in 3 stages; 84% cost reduction vs. prior methods at $2.33/paper with GPT-4o.

title: AI Can Learn Scientific Taste
authors: Jingqi Tong, Mingzhe Li, Hangcheng Li et al. (Fudan, OpenMOSS, Tsinghua)
contribution: Proposes RLCF (Reinforcement Learning from Community Feedback) using 700K citation-based paper pairs to train 'Scientific Judge' (outperforms GPT-5.2, Gemini 3 Pro at predicting paper impact) and 'Scientific Thinker' (generates higher-impact research ideas).

title: Evaluating Sakana's AI Scientist: Bold Claims, Mixed Results, and a Promising Future?
authors: Joeran Beel (U. Siegen), Min-Yen Kan (NUS), Moritz Baumgart
contribution: Independent evaluation finding 42% experiment failure rate due to coding errors, poor novelty assessment (misclassifying micro-batching as novel), and inability to critically assess own results.

title: The Ideation-Execution Gap: Execution Outcomes of LLM-Generated versus Human Research Ideas
authors: Chenglei Si, Tatsunori Hashimoto, Diyi Yang (Stanford)
contribution: 43 expert researchers each spent 100+ hours executing randomly-assigned ideas; AI ideas' novelty scores dropped dramatically after execution (-1.049 vs -0.010 for humans on 1-10 scale). AI ideas appear novel but collapse when implemented.

title: Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks
authors: T. Kwa, B. West, J. Becker, A. Deng et al. (METR)
contribution: Proposes '50%-task-completion time horizon' metric; finds frontier AI time horizon doubling approximately every 7 months since 2019. Extrapolation predicts 1-month task horizon between mid-2028 and mid-2031.

title: AI Researchers' Views on Automating AI R&D and Intelligence Explosions
authors: Severin Field, Raymond Douglas, David Krueger (MATS, Berkeley)
contribution: Interviewed 25 leading researchers from DeepMind, OpenAI, Anthropic, Meta, Berkeley, Princeton, Stanford; 20 of 25 (80%) identified automating AI research as one of the most severe and urgent AI risks.

title: OpenAI 'North Star' Announcement
authors: Jakub Pachocki (OpenAI Chief Scientist), MIT Technology Review interview
contribution: OpenAI's new 'North Star' is building a fully automated AI researcher. Timeline: autonomous 'AI research intern' by September 2026; fully automated multi-agent research system by 2028. Karpathy commented: 'All frontier LLM labs will do this -- it's the final BOSS battle.'

title: AI for Scientific Discovery is a Social Problem
contribution: Argues primary barriers to AI for science are social/institutional (not technical): community dysfunction, misaligned priorities, data fragmentation, infrastructure inequities.

title: Generative AI lacks the human creativity to achieve scientific discovery from scratch
contribution: Using molecular genetics lab simulation, found GenAI makes only incremental discoveries but cannot generate truly original hypotheses or detect anomalies; displays overconfident 'illusion of successful discovery.'

title: The Leaderboard Illusion
authors: Shivalika Singh et al. (Cohere, Princeton, Stanford, AI2, Washington)
contribution: Identifies systematic gaming of Chatbot Arena: Meta tested 27 private Llama-4 variants before public release; Google/OpenAI received ~19-20% of all arena data each vs. 29.7% for 83 open-weight models combined.

title: ICLR 2026: 21% AI-Generated Reviews (Pangram Labs / EditLens)
contribution: Of 75,800 ICLR 2026 peer reviews, 21% (15,899) flagged as fully AI-generated; >50% showed some AI involvement; only 43% purely human-written.

title: CycleResearcher: Improving Automated Research via Automated Review
authors: Yixuan Weng, Minjun Zhu, Guangsheng Bao et al.
contribution: Iterative framework where CycleResearcher generates papers and CycleReviewer simulates peer review with RL feedback; CycleReviewer achieves 26.89% MAE reduction vs. individual human reviewers.

title: ScienceAgentBench: Toward Rigorous Assessment of Language Agents for Data-Driven Scientific Discovery
contribution: 102 tasks from 44 peer-reviewed publications. 33.33% SOTA. Rigorous HAL-based evaluation framework.

title: PaperBench: Evaluating AI's Ability to Reproduce Research
contribution: 20 ICML 2024 papers, 8316 sub-tasks. Best agent: 27%; humans: 41%.

title: LAB-Bench: Measuring Capabilities of Language Models for Biology Research
contribution: Biology research capability evaluation. Most commonly used legacy benchmark in 2025-2026.

title: AgentRxiv: Collaborative preprint server for autonomous agent labs
contribution: Collaborative preprint server enabling autonomous agent labs to share research; 13.7% improvement over isolated labs on MATH-500.

title: NovelSeek: Unified closed-loop multi-agent framework for autonomous research
contribution: Unified closed-loop multi-agent framework for autonomous research across 12 tasks. Originally titled 'InternAgent.'

title: Benchmarking self-driving labs
contribution: Autonomous experiment/SDL metric system survey and benchmarking discussion.
